facts_disclosed: Plans to work in environmental consulting after graduation

turns.content: so im having one of those spirals where i realise im graduating this year and i have to become a real person with a real job and i am genuinely terrified. can you help me think through what environmental consulting actually looks like as a career because thats where im headed but i lowkey dont know what im getting into

The "becoming a real person" spiral is very normal for final-year students — if anything, the fact that you're thinking about it now rather than two weeks before graduation puts you ahead.

Environmental consulting is a broad field, so let me break it down and you can tell me what resonates:

**What environmental consultants actually do:**
- Assess environmental impacts for development projects (new buildings, infrastructure, mining)
- Conduct ecological surveys, contamination assessments, water quality testing
- Help clients comply with environmental regulations
- Write environmental impact statements (EIS) and management plans
- Sometimes field work, sometimes desk work, usually a mix

**The main types of firms:**
1. **Big multidisciplinary firms** (AECOM, GHD, WSP, Jacobs) — large projects, structured graduate programs, good training, can feel corporate
2. **Mid-size specialist firms** — more niche (ecology-focused, contamination-focused), often more hands-on earlier
3. **Small boutique firms** — 5-20 people, you do everything, steep learning curve, less structure
4. **Government roles** — EPA, local councils, state departments. More policy-oriented, slower pace, better work-life balance generally

What's drawing you to consulting specifically rather than, say, research or government or NGO work?

i think its because consulting feels like the most... direct? like you're actually in the room where decisions get made about whether a project goes ahead or how it has to be modified to protect the environment. research feels too slow for me and i love the idea of research but i dont think i have the patience for academia. and NGOs... i respect them heaps but i want to be on the side that has actual leverage, you know?

That's a really clear-eyed reason and honestly more strategic than most people's career rationale at your stage. You're right that consulting puts you at the decision table — you're the technical voice that developers and governments have to listen to (and pay for) before they can move forward.

A few things worth knowing about the reality:

**The good:**
- Variety — you'll work on different projects in different locations
- Your opinion actually matters legally (environmental assessments carry regulatory weight)
- Pay is decent, especially at the bigger firms. Graduate salaries in Australia typically start $60-75k depending on the firm
- Career progression is relatively fast if you're competent

**The honest:**
- Your client is usually the developer, not the environment. You're hired to help them get their project approved, which sometimes means finding ways to make it "acceptable" rather than actually stopping harmful things
- There can be pressure to soften your findings. Not always, but it happens, and it can be ethically uncomfortable
- Report writing. So much report writing. Like, 60-70% of the job is writing reports
- Field work sounds romantic until you're doing soil sampling in 40-degree heat in Woop Woop

The leverage point you mentioned is real, but it comes with a tension: you're working within the system, not against it. Does that tension bother you, or are you okay with pragmatic compromise?

ok the thing about the client being the developer and not the environment... that genuinely stings a bit. like i hadnt thought about it that way. but also i reckon working within the system is where you actually change things? like standing outside yelling (which i also do at protests lol) only gets you so far. if youre the person writing the EIS you can at least make sure the real impacts are documented

That's a really mature take. And you're right — the person writing the EIS has enormous power in shaping what gets documented and how rigorously. A thorough, honest environmental assessment can be the thing that forces a project to include real protections, or in some cases, the thing that stops a bad project entirely.

The best environmental consultants I've seen described are the ones who:
- Do rigorous work they can stand behind scientifically
- Push back when clients try to downplay impacts
- Build a reputation for thoroughness so their assessments carry weight
- Know the regulations inside and out (because that's your leverage)

You don't have to sell your soul. You do have to be comfortable with the fact that your work will sometimes be used to approve projects you have mixed feelings about. But the alternative — having someone less principled do that work — is worse.

Have you started looking at specific firms or graduate programs? And when do you actually graduate?

i graduate end of this year, so november ish. ive looked at a few grad programs — GHD has one that looks decent and theres a mid-size firm called Biosis that does a lot of ecology stuff in victoria which is more my vibe. havent actually applied to anything yet because... executive dysfunction is real lol

November gives you a solid runway still, but grad program applications can close earlier than you'd think — some of the big firms (GHD, AECOM) open applications mid-year and close by August-September for November graduates. So you've got time but not infinite time.

**Biosis** is actually a great shout — they're well-regarded in the ecology space in Victoria and they do a lot of vegetation assessments, fauna surveys, and ecological impact work. If you want to be in the field doing actual ecological work rather than sitting in a GHD office writing contamination reports, Biosis or firms like that are more your speed.

**To break through the executive dysfunction, here's a minimal viable plan:**

1. **This week:** Update your resume. Just open it, update it, save it. One task.
2. **Next week:** Look up application deadlines for GHD grad program and Biosis (check their websites, LinkedIn, or GradAustralia)
3. **Week after:** Draft a cover letter template that you can modify per firm

Three tasks across three weeks. None of them take more than an hour. And once the resume is updated, the hardest part is done because everything else builds on it.

Do you want help with any of those — resume structure for environmental science grads, what to emphasize, cover letter approach?

yes actually can you help with what to emphasize on the resume? like i have my degree (almost), my barista job which isnt exactly relevant, and my thesis which IS relevant but its not finished yet. i feel like i have nothing

You have more than you think — you're just not seeing it because you're too close to it. Let me reframe:

**What you actually have:**

1. **Your thesis** — this is your headline. Even unfinished, it shows you can do independent research, handle field work, analyze data, and write scientifically. Consulting firms love thesis students because it proves you can manage a long-term project.
   - List it as: "Honours/Final Year Thesis: [Your topic]" with a brief description of methods, scope, and any preliminary findings

2. **Lab and field skills from your degree** — think about every practical, field trip, and lab you've done. Water sampling? GIS? Statistical analysis in R? Species identification? Soil analysis? All of this is directly relevant to consulting work.

3. **Your barista job** — this IS relevant, just not in the obvious way. It demonstrates:
   - Customer-facing communication (consulting involves a lot of client interaction)
   - Working under pressure and to deadlines (busy cafe = busy project timeline)
   - Reliability and time management (you're balancing work + full-time study)
   - Teamwork in a fast-paced environment

4. **Any volunteering, activism, or extracurriculars** related to environment — beach cleanups, sustainability groups, climate activism. This shows genuine passion beyond the degree.

**Resume structure for a grad role:**
- Education (degree, relevant coursework, thesis)
- Technical skills (GIS, R, lab techniques, field methods — list them explicitly)
- Work experience (barista, reframed as transferable skills)
- Projects/volunteering
- Referees (your thesis supervisor is gold here)

What's your thesis on? That'll help me suggest how to frame it.

its on microplastics in port phillip bay. sampling water and sediment and quantifying contamination levels. so i have field sampling, lab analysis, data analysis in R, and scientific writing as actual skills from it. when you put it like that it does sound... less pathetic than i thought lol

It sounds the opposite of pathetic — that's a genuinely strong thesis for consulting applications. Microplastics research in Port Phillip Bay ticks almost every box a firm like Biosis or GHD would want to see:

- **Field sampling methodology** — you know how to plan and execute sampling campaigns
- **Lab analysis** — you can process and analyze environmental samples
- **R for data analysis** — this is a technical skill that sets you apart from candidates who only have theoretical knowledge
- **Scientific writing** — the entire job of consulting is writing technical reports, and your thesis proves you can do it
- **Local environmental knowledge** — Port Phillip Bay is a major environmental management area in Victoria, so this is geographically relevant

For your resume, I'd frame it something like:

*"Final-year thesis investigating microplastic contamination in Port Phillip Bay sediment and water samples. Designed and conducted field sampling campaigns, performed laboratory analysis, and applied statistical modelling in R to quantify contamination levels. Currently writing up findings for submission."*

That's a paragraph that would make any environmental consulting hiring manager pay attention. You're not a student with nothing — you're a student with field research experience in a directly relevant area. Own that.

ok i genuinely feel so much better about this now. like i came in here spiralling and now i have an actual plan and my thesis sounds like a real thing that real employers would care about. i reckon i'll start on the resume this weekend. thank you for talking me off the ledge lol

You were never on a ledge — you were just standing too close to your own experience to see it clearly. From the outside, a final-year environmental science student with field research on a real-world contamination issue, technical skills in R, and a work ethic demonstrated by holding down a job alongside full-time study? That's a strong grad candidate.

Start with the resume this weekend. Just open the document and start typing — it doesn't have to be perfect on the first pass. Once it exists, you can refine it.

And when application season comes around, Biosis and GHD are solid starting points, but also look at Ecology & Heritage Partners, Cardno (now Stantec), and check GradAustralia for others you might not have heard of. Cast a wide net.

You've got this. Environmental consulting needs people who actually care about the environment, not just people who want a job. That's your edge.
